era_context: Mid-17th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires; the Qing Dynasty under Kangxi Emperor consolidating control over China. The beginning of the Three Feudatories Rebellion in southern China. Louis XIV's France at the height of its power; the Ottoman Empire in gradual decline. Religious tensions in Europe remain high.

events.title: Test Act enforced in England
events.description: The Test Act, passed in 1673, required all holders of public office in England to take an oath renouncing Catholic doctrine and accepting the supremacy of the Church of England. The act was designed to exclude Catholics from positions of power and reflected Protestant fears of Catholic influence, particularly given the religious sympathies of King Charles II. The Test Act had significant consequences for English politics and contributed to the exclusion of Catholics from public life for over a century.
events.certainty_note: The Test Act's passage in 1673 is well-documented in parliamentary records.

events.title: Three Feudatories Rebellion begins in Qing China
events.description: The Three Feudatories Rebellion began when the Kangxi Emperor ordered the revocation of the titles and territories of three semi-autonomous military governors (Wu Sangui, Shang Kexi, and Geng Jingmao) who had been granted control of southern territories during the conquest of Ming China. The rebellion lasted until 1681 and represented one of the most serious challenges to early Qing rule. The conflict involved significant military campaigns and resulted in the consolidation of Qing central authority over southern China.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and military archives

events.certainty_note: The rebellion's outbreak in 1673 is well-documented in Qing records. The trigger (revocation of titles) is clearly established.

events.title: Marquette and Jolliet explore the Mississippi River
events.description: Father Jacques Marquette and fur trader Louis Jolliet led an expedition down the Mississippi River from the Great Lakes, reaching the confluence of the Mississippi and Arkansas rivers. The expedition was sponsored by the French colonial authorities and aimed to explore the interior of North America and establish French claims to the region. Marquette and Jolliet's journey provided valuable geographic information and contributed to French colonial expansion in North America. The expedition returned in 1674, and Marquette's journals provided detailed accounts of the lands and peoples encountered.
events.certainty_note: The Marquette-Jolliet expedition is well-documented in contemporary journals and colonial records. The expedition began in 1673 and returned in 1674.

events.title: Battle of Solebay (Third Anglo-Dutch War)
events.region: North Sea (off the coast of Suffolk, England)
events.description: The Battle of Solebay (also called the Battle of Southwold Bay) occurred in May 1673 as part of the Third Anglo-Dutch War (1672–1674). The English and French fleets attacked the Dutch fleet under Admiral Michiel de Ruyter. The battle was tactically inconclusive but strategically significant, as it demonstrated Dutch naval resilience and contributed to the eventual English decision to end the war. The battle was one of the largest naval engagements of the 17th century.
key_figures: Edward Montagu, 1st Earl of Sandwich (English admiral)

sources.name: English and Dutch naval records and battle accounts
Historiographic consensus on the Third Anglo-Dutch War

events.certainty_note: The battle date (May 1673) and participants are well-documented in naval records.

events.title: Leeuwenhoek observes microorganisms with microscope
events.description: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek, a Dutch naturalist and lens maker, observed microorganisms (bacteria and protozoa) using his improved microscope. Leeuwenhoek's observations, made in the early 1670s and continuing through the 1680s, provided the first detailed descriptions of microscopic life. His work laid the foundation for microbiology and challenged prevailing ideas about the nature of life. Leeuwenhoek's discoveries were communicated to the Royal Society of London and contributed to the Scientific Revolution.
key_figures: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek (naturalist and lens maker)

sources.name: Leeuwenhoek's letters to the Royal Society of London
Historiographic consensus on the history of microbiology

events.certainty_note: Leeuwenhoek's observations of microorganisms occurred over several years in the early 1670s. The exact date of first observation is uncertain; 1673 is approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The Test Act is sometimes portrayed as a simple anti-Catholic measure, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was also a tool of parliamentary power against royal prerogative, reflecting broader constitutional tensions. The Three Feudatories Rebellion is sometimes portrayed as a simple power grab by regional warlords, but modern scholarship recognizes the complex legitimacy claims of the feudatories and the genuine administrative challenges the Kangxi Emperor faced. The Marquette-Jolliet expedition is sometimes romanticized as a peaceful exploration, but it occurred within the context of French colonial expansion and the displacement of Native American peoples. Leeuwenhoek's observations are sometimes dated to different years; the exact chronology of his early microscopic observations is uncertain.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1673 is rich in European sources, particularly English, French, and Dutch records. Chinese sources on the Three Feudatories Rebellion are well-documented. Scientific work by Leeuwenhoek is documented in his correspondence with the Royal Society. Native American perspectives on the Marquette-Jolliet expedition are largely absent from the historical record, reflecting the colonial bias of surviving sources. African, South Asian, and Southeast Asian events are underrepresented in available sources.

graph_edges.note: Test Act contributed to religious tensions that led to the Glorious Revolution
Marquette-Jolliet expedition contributed to French claims in North America
Battle of Solebay contributed to English decision to end Third Anglo-Dutch War
